name: San Clemente - Mecerreyes Loop
name_original: Mecerreyes por el Camino del Cid — circular desde Cuevas de San Clemente
description: Jog the San Clemente - Mecerreyes Loop, a moderate 8.2-mile (13.2 km) route in Spain with 867 feet (264 metres) of elevation gain.
region_intro: Jogging routes around Cuevas De San Clemente traverse a landscape characterized by its inclusion within the Sabinares del Arlanza Natural Park, featuring the world's largest juniper forests, oak woodlands, and diverse riverside vegetation along the Arlanza River. The terrain offers a mix of rural roads, forested paths, and moderate inclines, situated between the Sierra de la Demanda and Sierra de las Mamblas mountain ranges. Limestone gorges like La Yecla add geological interest to the area's natural features. Best jogging routes around Cuevas De San Clemente The most popular jogging route is Camino del Cid from Covarrubias — circular through the Sabinares de Arlanza Natural Park - La Yecla, a 10.1 miles (16.3 km) trail that takes 1 hour 56 minutes to complete. This difficult route explores sections of the historic Camino del Cid and the natural park. Another top favourite among local runners is San Clemente - Mecerreyes Loop, a moderate 8.2 miles (13.2 km) path. This route offers varied terrain and takes approximately 1 hour 39 minutes to complete. Local runners also love the Historic Center of Covarrubias – Casa de Doña Sancha loop from Covarrubias, a 14.7 miles (23.7 km) trail leading through forested areas and historic sites, often completed in about 3 hours 2 minutes. Jogging around Cuevas De San Clemente is defined by ancient juniper forests, limestone gorges, and rural roads. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate loops to more difficult, longer running routes. The guide includes highly-rated routes that showcase the best of the region's landscape. More than 20 runners have used komoot to explore Cuevas De San Clemente's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many running routes are available around Cuevas De San Clemente?
A: There are over 7 dedicated running routes around Cuevas De San Clemente, offering a variety of distances and difficulty levels to suit different preferences. These routes traverse diverse landscapes, from ancient juniper forests to rural roads.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on the jogging trails in this region?
A: The terrain around Cuevas De San Clemente is quite varied. You'll find a mix of rural roads with firm ground, forested paths, and moderate inclines, especially in areas like the Sierra de las Mamblas. The region is characterized by its inclusion within the Sabinares del Arlanza Natural Park, featuring ancient juniper forests and diverse riverside vegetation.

Q: Are there any easy or beginner-friendly running paths in Cuevas De San Clemente?
A: While many routes offer moderate to difficult challenges, the area does have options suitable for beginners. For instance, the local &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38275197" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Camino del Cid loop from Barrio del Arrabal&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate option that provides a good introduction to the region's trails without extreme difficulty.

Q: Can I find circular running routes in the area?
A: Yes, several circular running routes are available. A notable one is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1050096144/camino-del-cid-from-covarrubias-circular-through-the-sabinares-de-arlanza-natural-park-la-yecla" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Camino del Cid from Covarrubias — circular through the Sabinares de Arlanza Natural Park - La Yecla&lt;/a&gt;, which offers a challenging loop through the natural park. Another option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1050108949/san-clemente-mecerreyes-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;San Clemente - Mecerreyes Loop&lt;/a&gt;, providing a moderate circular path.

Q: What natural features or landmarks can I see while running in Cuevas De San Clemente?
A: The routes often pass through the &lt;strong&gt;Sabinares del Arlanza Natural Park&lt;/strong&gt;, known for its ancient juniper forests. You might also encounter geological wonders like &lt;strong&gt;La Yecla Gorge&lt;/strong&gt;, a deep limestone canyon. Some trails offer views of the Sierra de las Mamblas. For specific landmarks, consider routes near &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4659990" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Historic Center of Covarrubias&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5454929" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Statue of El Cid in Mecerreyes&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are the running trails in Cuevas De San Clemente suitable for families?
A: Many of the trails are suitable for families, especially those looking for a moderate outdoor activity. Shorter, well-defined paths like the local &lt;strong&gt;Camino de San Clemente&lt;/strong&gt; (approx. 1.5 km) from the village to the San Clemente fountain can be a good starting point for families, with options to extend the run on parts of the PRC-BU 216 route.

Q: Are dogs allowed on the jogging routes?
A: Generally, dogs are welcome on most outdoor trails in the region, especially within natural parks like Sabinares del Arlanza. However, it's always advisable to keep them on a leash to protect local wildlife and ensure a pleasant experience for all users. Please check specific park regulations if you plan to visit protected areas.

Q: Where can I park my car when accessing the running trails?
A: Parking is typically available in or near the villages that serve as starting points for the trails, such as Cuevas De San Clemente itself or nearby Covarrubias. For specific routes, look for designated parking areas at trailheads or within the nearest town centers.

Q: What is the best time of year to go running in Cuevas De San Clemente?
A: The spring and autumn months generally offer the most pleasant conditions for running, with mild temperatures and vibrant natural scenery. Summer can be warm, so early morning or late afternoon runs are recommended. Winter running is possible, but be prepared for cooler temperatures and potentially some frost, especially at higher elevations.

Q: Are there any routes that offer good viewpoints?
A: Yes, routes that ascend into the Sierra de las Mamblas, such as the &lt;strong&gt;Camino de las Mamblas - Ermita&lt;/strong&gt;, offer rewarding views of Cuevas De San Clemente and the surrounding landscape from higher ground. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/24550112" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Historic Center of Covarrubias – Casa de Doña Sancha loop from Covarrubias&lt;/a&gt; also provides scenic vistas through forested areas.

Q: How long do the running routes typically take to complete?
A: The duration varies significantly by route and individual pace. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1050096144/camino-del-cid-from-covarrubias-circular-through-the-sabinares-de-arlanza-natural-park-la-yecla" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Camino del Cid from Covarrubias&lt;/a&gt;, a difficult 16.3 km trail, typically takes around 1 hour 56 minutes. A moderate 13.2 km path like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1050108949/san-clemente-mecerreyes-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;San Clemente - Mecerreyes Loop&lt;/a&gt; might take about 1 hour 39 minutes.
tips: Beautiful hermitage recently built, due to the special devotion to the Virgen del Camino that the inhabitants of Mecerreyes have.
The origin of Mecerreyes, is in all probability, etymologically Arabic origin (maza rais) which means meadow or field. Its inhabitants are known as guiletos, a name that comes from guilla (harvest).
What is called Camino del Cid we find a large number of field extensions that give a panoramic view of the set of spaces that exist in the area.

faqs.question: How long does it take to jog this loop?
faqs.answer: This jogging route, officially named "Mecerreyes along the Camino del Cid circular from Cuevas de San Clemente", is approximately 13.2 km long. It typically takes about 1 hour and 39 minutes to complete.

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level of this jogging route?
faqs.answer: The route is rated as moderate. It involves an elevation gain of about 264 meters, making it a good challenge for regular joggers.

faqs.question: What can I see along the route?
faqs.answer: You'll pass through the charming settlement of Mecerreyes and encounter the historic Hermitage of Our Lady of the Way. A significant portion of the route also follows the famous Camino del Cid.

faqs.question: Where does the trail start and is there parking available?
faqs.answer: This circular route starts and ends in Cuevas de San Clemente. While specific parking details aren't provided, you can typically find parking in or near the village center of Cuevas de San Clemente, which serves as the trailhead.

faqs.question: Is this jogging route suitable for families?
faqs.answer: Given its moderate difficulty and 13.2 km length, this route is best suited for families with older children who are accustomed to jogging or longer walks. Younger children might find it too challenging.

faqs.question: Is the route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route is generally dog-friendly. However, always keep your dog on a leash, especially when passing through settlements or near livestock, and be sure to carry water for them.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to visit?
faqs.answer: Spring and autumn are generally the best seasons for jogging this route. The weather is usually milder, and the natural scenery is vibrant. Summers can be hot, and winters may bring colder temperatures and potentially challenging conditions.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required?
faqs.answer: No, there are no permits or entrance fees required to access this public jogging route.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route incorporates sections of several official trails, including the Sendero de San Clemente, Sendero de la Cuna del Buen Conde, and significant portions of the Camino del Cid (both Senderista and BTT versions), as well as the GR 160 - Camino del Cid BTT.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like for jogging?
faqs.answer: The terrain is varied, typical of rural Spanish paths. You can expect a mix of dirt tracks, gravel paths, and possibly some paved sections as you pass through villages. The elevation changes contribute to the moderate difficulty.

faqs.question: Are there any facilities like cafes or accommodation nearby?
faqs.answer: You will find basic facilities, including potential cafes or small shops, in the villages along the route, particularly in Cuevas de San Clemente and Mecerreyes. Accommodation options might also be available in these settlements.

waypoints.name: Hermitage of Our Lady of the Way
